1: Shimou devar-YHWH benei Yisrael -- 'Hear the word of YHWH, O children of Israel.' The riv (covenant lawsuit) genre is announced: YHWH has a case against the land's inhabitants. Ki riv la-YHWH im-yoshvei ha-aretz -- 'for YHWH has a case against the inhabitants of the land.' Ki ein emet ve-ein chesed ve-ein daath Elohim ba-aretz -- 'for there is no faithfulness (emet), no covenant loyalty (chesed), and no knowledge of God (daath Elohim) in the land.' The three covenant virtues are listed as absent. Emet (H571, truth/faithfulness) -- dependability in covenant relationship. Chesed (H2617, covenant love/loyalty) -- the quality YHWH repeatedly displays and Israel fails to match. Daath Elohim (knowledge of God) -- the relational intimacy and covenantal awareness that Hosea has established as the center of YHWH's desire (Hosea 6:6). All three are missing simultaneously: the social, relational, and theological dimensions of covenant life have collapsed.
2: Aloh ve-kachesh ve-ratzoch ve-ganav ve-naof -- 'There is cursing, lying, murder, stealing, and adultery.' The five sins roughly parallel the second table of the Decalogue (commandments 6-9, with oaths added). Paratzto -- 'they break all bounds.' Ve-damim be-damim nagau -- 'and bloodshed follows bloodshed.' The image of blood touching blood: violence so pervasive that each act of murder creates the conditions for the next. The social fabric that the covenant was designed to protect has been torn through completely. The lawsuits and moral failures are not isolated incidents but a systemic collapse.
3: Al-ken teeval ha-aretz -- 'Therefore the land mourns.' Aval (H56, to mourn/wither) -- the land itself participates in grief at the covenant violation. The covenant is not merely a human-divine agreement but has cosmic dimensions: the land's fruitfulness and the creation order are bound up with covenant fidelity (cf. Leviticus 26:20; Jeremiah 12:4). Ve-umlag kol-yoshev bah -- 'and all who live in it waste away.' Be-chayat ha-sadeh u-ve-of ha-shamayim ve-gam degei ha-yam yeasaphu -- 'together with the animals of the field, the birds of the sky, and even the fish of the sea.' The three realms of creation (land, sky, sea) are all affected. The ecological collapse is the covenant curse made visible in the natural world -- an anticipation of the creation-groaning theology of Romans 8:19-22.
4: Akh ish al-yarev ve-al-yochiach ish -- 'Yet let no one accuse or rebuke another.' The irony of a society in which lawsuit is the dominant mode of interaction (Hosea 4:1, riv) yet no one has standing to accuse another -- because all are equally guilty. Ve-ammekha ki-merivei kohen -- 'for your people are like those who bring charges against a priest.' The sacerdotal legal immunity -- or the inversion of the priestly role from teacher to defendant -- the priest who should adjudicate the law has become its transgressor. The verse is difficult grammatically and has multiple interpretive options; the MKT follows the reading that the people's condition resembles constant litigation against their own leaders.
5: Ve-kashalta ha-yom ve-kashalta gam-navi immekha lailah -- 'You stumble in the daylight; the prophet stumbles with you in the night.' The stumblings of the people and the prophet are simultaneous: both are failing. Ve-dammiti immekha -- 'and I will destroy your mother.' The 'mother' is the national community -- Israel as mother of the individual Israelites (cf. Hosea 2:2). The priests and prophets who should have provided guidance through the darkness are themselves stumbling.
6: Nidmu ammi mi-beli ha-daath -- 'My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge.' The most famous verse in Hosea. Nidam (H1826, destroyed/silenced/cut off) -- total annihilation. The cause: mi-beli ha-daath (for lack of knowledge). Daath (H1847, knowledge) is the relational knowing that the covenant relationship produces -- not merely information but experiential intimacy with YHWH and his Torah. Ki attah ha-daath maasta -- 'Because you have rejected knowledge.' The rejection is active: Israel did not merely fail to acquire knowledge, she pushed it away. Va-emmaasekha mi-kehannei li -- 'I will reject you from being my priest.' The consequence is proportionate: those who reject the priestly vocation of teaching Torah are themselves rejected from the priesthood. Ve-tishkach Torat Elohekha -- 'and since you have forgotten the Torah of your God' -- the active forgetting of Torah is the mechanism of the knowledge-failure.
7: Ke-rabbam ken chatu-li -- 'The more they multiplied, the more they sinned against me.' The paradox of prosperity and sin: the growth of the population (the Abrahamic blessing) became the occasion for multiplied transgression rather than multiplied fidelity. Kevodim be-qalon amir -- 'they exchanged their glory for shame.' Kavod (H3519, glory/honor) -- the glory of the covenant relationship with YHWH; qalon (H7036, shame/dishonor). The exchange formula is the prophetic vocabulary for idolatry: trading the genuine article for an inferior substitute (cf. Jeremiah 2:11; Romans 1:23). What was glorious has been traded for something shameful.
8: Chatat ammi yokhelu -- 'They feed on the sins of my people.' The priests' economic dependence on the sin offering (chatat, H2403) has created a perverse incentive: more sin = more priestly income. Ve-el avonam yissu nafsham -- 'they are greedy for their iniquity.' The priestly institution that was supposed to mediate forgiveness has become structurally committed to perpetuating the sin that funds it. This is a systemic critique of corrupt institutionalized religion: the very mechanisms of atonement become the instruments of exploitation when the priests profit from transgression.
9: Ve-hayah ke-am ke-kohen -- 'And it will be: like people, like priest.' The moral leveling: the priestly distinction is dissolved by the priests' participation in the people's sin. U-faqadti alav derakaiv -- 'I will punish them both for their ways.' Ve-ma'alalehem ashiv lo -- 'and repay them for their deeds.' The lex talionis principle of covenant justice: the punishment mirrors the sin. When priests become indistinguishable from the sinful people, they share the people's judgment.
10: Ve-akhlu ve-lo yisbau -- 'They will eat but not be satisfied.' The covenant curse of unfulfillment: abundance without satisfaction (Leviticus 26:26; Micah 6:14). Hiznu ve-lo yifrotzu -- 'they will pursue prostitution but not flourish.' The fertility-cult logic fails: they pursue the Baals for fertility and get barrenness. Ki et-YHWH azvu -- 'because they have abandoned the LORD.' The abandonment of YHWH is the single cause of the double curse: unsatisfied hunger and barren sexuality.
11: Zenut ve-yayin ve-tirosh yiqach-lev -- 'Prostitution, wine, and new wine take away the understanding.' Lev (H3820, heart/mind/understanding) -- the cognitive and moral center. Three intoxicants: sexual immorality, old wine, and new wine. The loss of lev is the loss of the capacity for daath -- the knowledge that Hosea holds as the central covenant virtue. Moral intoxication removes the very faculty needed for covenant fidelity.
12: Ammi be-etzo yishal -- 'My people consult a piece of wood.' The absurdity of wood-divination: a stick answers the questions that Torah should answer. Ve-maqlo yagid lo -- 'and their divining rod gives them answers.' Ki ruach zenutim hitah -- 'for a spirit of prostitution has led them astray.' Ruach zenutim (spirit of prostitution/unfaithfulness) -- not merely a social tendency but a spiritual power that drives Israel away from YHWH. The 'spirit of prostitution' appears three times in Hosea (Hosea 4:12; Hosea 5:4) as the animating principle of Israel's idolatry. Va-yiznu mi-tachat Eloheihem -- 'and they have played the whore away from under their God.' The preposition mi-tachat (from under) -- they were under YHWH's covenant authority and have departed from under it.
13: Al-rashei he-harim yezabechu ve-al-ha-gevot yeqatteru -- 'They sacrifice on the mountaintops and burn incense on the hills.' The high-place cult: mountaintop worship was the standard form of Canaanite religion that Israel absorbed. Tachat alon vi-livneh ve-elah ki tov tzilah -- 'under oak and poplar and terebinth trees, because the shade is good.' The practical attraction of shade and elevation -- religion accommodated to physical comfort. Al-ken tiznena benoteikem ve-khaloteikem tena'afnah -- 'therefore your daughters commit prostitution, and your daughters-in-law commit adultery.' The social consequence of cultic prostitution: the normalization of sexual immorality at the sanctuary spreads to the household. The cult's sexual sin is not contained in the sacred space but shapes the entire social fabric.
14: Lo-afqod al-benoteikem ki tiznena -- 'I will not punish your daughters when they commit prostitution.' The seeming double standard is explained: Ve-ha-anashim im-ha-zedeshot yefalelu -- 'because the men themselves go off with the cult prostitutes.' The men who frequent the cult prostitutes cannot then punish the daughters who become cult prostitutes. The male leadership's participation in the cult is the cause of the female population's sexual sin. YHWH will not punish the symptom without addressing the cause. Ve-am lo-yavin yillavet -- 'a people without understanding comes to ruin.' The absence of binah (understanding) -- related to daath -- is the root cause of the social-moral collapse.
15: Im-zoneh attah Yisrael -- 'Though you, Israel, commit adultery.' The northern kingdom's apostasy is addressed with a warning to Judah: do not be infected by the northern idolatry. Al-yeasham Yehudah -- 'do not let Judah become guilty.' Ve-al-tavu ha-Gilgal -- 'do not go to Gilgal.' Gilgal was a legitimate cultic site that had become associated with syncretism (cf. Amos 5:5). Ve-al-talu Beit-Aven -- 'or journey up to Beth-aven.' Beth-aven (house of wickedness/futility) is Hosea's contemptuous renaming of Beth-el (house of God, H1008) -- the northern sanctuary established by Jeroboam I with its golden calves (1 Kings 12:29). The sacred name has been desecrated by the false cult.
16: Ki ke-farah sareirah sarer Yisrael -- 'For Israel has become stubborn, like a stubborn heifer.' Sareirah (H5637, stubborn/obstinate) -- the animal image of the ox that refuses the yoke. In Deuteronomy, the 'stiff-necked' (qesheh-oref) people resist divine direction. The heifer that will not yield to the harness cannot be used to plow -- she is useless for the covenant labor. Attah yirenhem YHWH ke-keves be-merchav -- 'Can the LORD now pasture them like a lamb in open country?' The rhetorical question implies that a stubborn heifer cannot be safely pastured -- the covenant relationship of divine shepherding requires a responsive flock.
17: Chavur atzabbim Efrayim -- 'Ephraim is attached to idols.' Chavar (H2266, to be joined/attached/bound) -- the bond to idols is as strong as the covenant bond to YHWH should be, but directed at the wrong object. Hanacha lo -- 'leave him alone.' The divine withdrawal: YHWH steps back from striving with Ephraim and allows the full consequences of idolatry to unfold. The 'leave him alone' anticipates Paul's description of divine judgment as 'God gave them up' (Romans 1:24, 26, 28) -- the withdrawal of restraint as the beginning of judgment.
18: Sar savaam -- 'Their drinking bouts are over.' The translation is uncertain (sar can mean 'ruler/prince' or 'to turn aside'); the MKT reads the carousing that has characterized the cult as coming to an end. Hiznu hiznah -- 'they have given themselves completely to prostitution.' Ahevu hev hevish magineha -- 'their rulers dearly love shame.' The political leadership's love of the shameful practices makes the corruption systemic from the top down. What the priests do (Hosea 4:8-10), the rulers amplify.
19: Tzarar ruach otah bi-khanafehah -- 'A wind will sweep them away in its wings.' Ruach (H7307, wind/spirit) -- the same word used for the 'spirit of prostitution' in Hosea 4:12; now the wind is the instrument of judgment, sweeping away the idolaters in its wings. Ve-yevoshu mi-zivchotam -- 'and they will be ashamed of their sacrifices.' The shame that idolatry promised to avoid (social disgrace, cultic impurity) is precisely what idolatry delivers. The sacrifices at the high places, which were meant to win the Baals' favor, become the objects of shame when the Baals prove powerless.
